sections.heading: KEY REGULATORY INSTRUMENTS FOR THE ACTUALIZATION OF CLOUD
sections.title: Regulatory instruments for cloud computing implementation
sections.summary: This section identifies key regulatory instruments necessary for cloud computing implementation in Nigeria. It establishes the framework for cloud adoption by public institutions and SMEs, emphasizing the need for policy alignment with national digital strategies. The section outlines the role of NITDA in issuing cloud-related policies and the importance of regulatory compliance for effective cloud deployment.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the regulatory context for cloud computing in Nigeria, which is relevant for DTIAs as it establishes the policy framework governing cloud service providers and public sector adoption. It does not specify technical transfer mechanisms or data handling requirements.

requirements.text: Key regulatory instruments for cloud computing implementation must be established

sections.source_quote: KEY REGULATORY INSTRUMENTS FOR THE ACTUALIZATION OF CLOUD 23.0

sections.title: Cloud computing policy implementation framework
sections.summary: This section details the implementation framework for cloud computing in Nigeria, targeting public institutions and SMEs. It mandates a 'cloud-first' approach for public sector agencies and SMEs to adopt cloud services, with specific objectives to increase cloud adoption by 30% and cloud investments by 35% by 2024. The policy requires public sector entities to prioritize cloud-based procurement of ICT solutions to reduce costs and improve service delivery.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates a 'cloud-first' approach for public sector cloud adoption, which impacts DTIAs by requiring assessments of cloud service providers' compliance with national data governance frameworks. It does not specify data transfer mechanisms or cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: Public sector entities must prioritize procurement of cloud-based ICT solutions
Federal Public Institutions must achieve 30% cloud adoption by 2024
Cloud computing investments must increase by 35% by 2024
Cloud service providers must be local or international providers rendering services to FPIs and SMEs

sections.source_quote: The Nigeria Cloud Computing Policy is issued pursuant to Section 6 (a) (b) (c) and (i) of the National Information Technology Development Act 2007.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cloud service models and deployment options for public institutions
sections.summary: This section describes cloud computing service models (SaaS, PaaS, IaaS) and deployment models (private, public, hybrid clouds) applicable to Nigerian public institutions. It outlines how these models enable efficient service delivery, cost savings, and resource optimization while addressing security and operational needs through standardized frameworks. Public institutions must select models aligned with their specific requirements and security classifications.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines cloud service models and deployment options that impact data transfer and storage across borders. Public institutions must ensure cloud providers meet Nigerian data classification and security standards to prevent cross-border data risks.

requirements.text: Public institutions must select cloud service models (SaaS, PaaS, IaaS) based on their specific needs
Cloud service providers must not manage underlying infrastructure for SaaS
Cloud service providers must not manage underlying infrastructure for PaaS
Cloud service providers must not manage underlying infrastructure for IaaS
Public institutions must ensure cloud deployments align with Nigerian e-Government Interoperability Framework

sections.source_quote: The policy recognizes three basic kinds of cloud computing service offerings or cloud based service models as: i. Software as a Service (SaaS): where the consumer uses the provider's applications running on cloud infrastructure. ii. Platform as a Service (PaaS): PaaS capability is provided to consumer is a pre-installed cloud infrastructure platform... iii. Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS): The consumer can provision processing, storage, networks...

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cross-border data flows and classification requirements for cloud services
sections.summary: This section addresses cross-border data flows in cloud environments, requiring public institutions to ensure cloud service providers store data in jurisdictions with equivalent data protection standards to Nigeria. It mandates data classification based on sensitivity levels (public, confidential, secret, classified) with specific storage and security requirements for each category, including encryption and access controls. Public institutions must contract providers that meet these classifications to avoid compliance risks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies cross-border data transfer restrictions for cloud services, requiring public institutions to ensure data stored in jurisdictions with equivalent protection standards to Nigeria. It mandates data classification with strict storage requirements for sensitive data to prevent unauthorized access.

requirements.text: Public institutions must contract cloud service providers that store data in jurisdictions with equivalent data protection standards to Nigeria
Confidential data must reside primarily within Nigerian territorial boundaries
Secret data must reside primarily within Nigerian territorial boundaries
Classified data must reside only within Nigerian territorial boundaries
All data must be classified according to sensitivity levels (public, confidential, secret, classified)
Cloud service providers must implement encryption for data at rest and in motion
Public institutions must ensure strong user authentication for sensitive data

sections.source_quote: Agencies must do so in line with requirements of Nigerian Data Protection Regulation and any other Content Regulation. Federal Public Institutions shall be advised to contract cloud service providers that will store data in a jurisdiction that provides a level of personal data protection that is equivalent to that provided in Nigeria.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cloud service contracts for government entities require minimum data protection clauses
sections.summary: This section mandates that NITDA develops a regulatory framework for cloud contracts between public institutions and cloud service providers (CSPs). The framework ensures government entities receive equivalent rights to private customers and includes minimum contractual requirements such as CSP due diligence compliance, service descriptions, contract duration, payment terms, SLAs, data handling procedures, data retrieval rights upon termination, liability limitations, and security certifications. These provisions establish baseline contractual obligations for secure cloud data management.
sections.dtia_summary: Government entities must include specific contractual clauses in cloud agreements to ensure data protection, including data retrieval rights upon termination and liability limitations for data breaches. These requirements directly impact cross-border data transfers if government entities use international CSPs.

requirements.text: CSP must adhere to due diligence process and public procurement guidelines
Contract must specify service descriptions
Contract must define duration unless unlimited
Contract must include payment terms and termination clauses
Contract must detail Service Level Agreements (SLAs)
Contract must specify data handling procedures including processing, destruction and restoration
Contract must include customer care services depending on service offering
Contract must guarantee customer right to retrieve data upon termination
CSP must limit liability for unreasonable exclusion or unfair contract terms
Contract must specify cloud security and privacy levels

sections.source_quote: NITDA will work on the development of a regulatory framework for the execution of cloud computing contracts between Public Institutions and Cloud Service Providers (CSP). This regulatory framework shall ensure that government entities using the cloud as cloud customers enjoy at least the same rights as those enjoyed by individual customers, enterprises and other cloud customers.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cloud governance framework for public sector with four operational levels
sections.summary: This section establishes a four-level cloud governance framework for Nigerian public sector entities: infrastructure, operating system, platform/application, and business/user activity. It defines specific responsibilities for NITDA, Bureau of Public Procurement, ONSA, and other bodies to ensure cloud adoption aligns with security, compliance, and operational requirements. The framework includes roles for cloud readiness assessments, procurement regulation development, security monitoring, and coordination of cloud standards to manage risks and ensure accountability across all cloud operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Public sector entities must implement a governance structure with defined roles to manage cloud operations across four levels, ensuring security, compliance, and accountability for data processing and storage. This impacts data transfer mechanisms and cross-border data handling if international CSPs are used.

requirements.text: Bureau of Public Procurement must develop government-wide cloud procurement regulations
NITDA must liaise with BPP to accommodate cloud procurement models
NITDA must conduct cloud readiness assessments
ONSA and NITDA must monitor operational security issues related to cloud
NITDA must drive government-wide cloud adoption and identify next-generation technologies
NITDA must coordinate cloud-related activities across governance bodies
NITDA must monitor and prioritize cloud standards from NIST, ISO and other international bodies

sections.source_quote: The Bureau of Public Procurement shall develop and operationalize government-wide procurement regulation for Cloud services in consultation with NITDA and other relevant agencies of government
